assumptions: The fact pattern is assessed in consolidated financial statements of a non-investment-entity group.
The intragroup dividend has already been declared and a receivable/payable has been recognised, so the live issue is an existing intragroup monetary item rather than only a forecast transaction.
Foreign exchange exposure exists only if the relevant group entities have different functional currencies and the dividend balance is denominated in a currency that creates exchange differences.

approaches.label: General intragroup hedged-item prohibition
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items generally must involve an external party. Intragroup transactions are normally not eligible as hedged items.
approaches.practical_implication: Start from a presumption of ineligibility unless a specific IFRS 9 exception applies.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 says only items with a party external to the reporting entity can generally be designated in consolidation, subject to limited exceptions.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of an intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 permits, as an exception, the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to be a hedged item in consolidation if the resulting FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: The recognised dividend balance is a monetary receivable/payable
The relevant group entities have different functional currencies
The FX gains or losses on that intragroup monetary item are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The hedge meets IFRS 9 designation, documentation and effectiveness requirements
approaches.practical_implication: A recognised foreign-currency intragroup dividend receivable may be hedge-eligible, but only for qualifying FX risk that survives consolidation.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements when FX effects are not fully eliminated.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation and effectiveness assessment for any qualifying hedge relationship.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of a forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 has a separate exception for highly probable forecast intragroup transactions whose FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss. That is not the primary model once a dividend receivable has already been recognised.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not analyse an already recognised dividend receivable under the forecast-transaction hedge model.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 6.3.6, 6.3.3 and B6.3.5-B6.3.6 permit certain highly probable forecast intragroup transactions to be hedged only when their FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge model
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 addresses hedges of the foreign currency risk of a net investment in a foreign operation, ie an amount of net assets. A separate intragroup dividend receivable is not that hedged item model.
approaches.practical_implication: A dividend receivable should not be justified as eligible by analogy to a net investment hedge.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 10-12 and 14 frame hedge accounting for net investments as applying to foreign currency risk on the net assets of a foreign operation, not to ordinary intragroup receivables.
